draft.introduction: In Enneads III.2.1, Plotinus addresses the problem of providence: how can a cosmos governed by divine intelligence contain evil, injustice, and suffering? His answer is that the whole is perfect even if the parts appear defective when viewed in isolation.

translation.text: Those who think that the world of creation is the work of an evil creator or of a mindless chance are refuted by the order and beauty of the cosmos. But we must also account for the existence of evil within it. Providence does not mean that everything should be good; rather, the whole contains all grades of being, from the highest to the lowest, and the lowest is matter—the limit of the soul’s descent.
translation.original_text: τὴν πρόνοιαν οὐ τοιαύτην εἶναι δεῖ, ἵνα μηδὲν ἦι παρ᾽ αὐτήν

translator_notes.note: Plotinus’s theodicy is aesthetic: evil exists because a cosmos with every grade of being is more complete than one containing only the highest. This “plenitude” argument was adopted by Augustine and became the dominant theodicy in Western theology.
